draft.introduction: Psalm 73 is an Asaph psalm wrestling with the prosperity of the wicked, moving from near-collapse of faith to restored vision in God’s sanctuary. Its conclusion—“Whom have I in heaven but you?”—is one of the Old Testament’s most intimate expressions of devotion.

translation.text: Truly God is good to Israel, to those who are pure in heart. But as for me, my feet had almost stumbled. For I was envious of the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked. Whom have I in heaven but you? And there is nothing on earth that I desire besides you. My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever.
translation.original_text: ὡς ἀγαθὸς ὁ θεὸς τῷ Ισραηλ τοῖς εὐθέσι τῇ καρδίᾳ

translator_notes.note: The confession that God is the psalmist’s “portion” (μερίς) forever transcends the problem of theodicy by redefining the good: the ultimate good is not earthly prosperity but God himself. Augustine built his theology of desire on this insight.
